---
title: "15 · Divisibility and Primes"
subtitle: "Numbers that can't be broken — and why the internet depends on them"
lesson-id: "t1.divisibility-primes"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.divide-fair-share"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["encryption", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute $35 \div 5 = 7$ and recognise that $35 \div 4$ has a
   remainder?
3. (Optional, but rewarding) Have you done
   [Lesson 11 — Variables](../11-intro-variables/index.qmd)? You'll
   appreciate the *general* statements more if so.
:::

## Touch

Pick a number $n$. The widget shows all its **divisors** (numbers that
divide $n$ exactly with no remainder) and tells you whether $n$ is
**prime**.

```{ojs}
viewof nVal = Inputs.range([2, 60], {value: 12, step: 1, label: "n:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const divisors = [];
  for (let i = 1; i <= nVal; i++) if (nVal % i === 0) divisors.push(i);
  const isPrime = divisors.length === 2;
  const W = 720, H = 220, pad = 30;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px; background:#fafafa; border:1px solid #e0e0e0; border-radius:6px;");

  // Display number
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W/2).attr("y", 50)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "48").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .attr("fill", isPrime ? "#c2185b" : "#1f2933")
    .text(nVal);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W/2).attr("y", 78)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "16")
    .attr("fill", isPrime ? "#c2185b" : "#666")
    .text(isPrime ? "PRIME" : `composite (${divisors.length} divisors)`);

  // Divisors row
  svg.append("text").attr("x", W/2).attr("y", 115)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#2c5f7c")
    .text("Divisors:");
  const dW = Math.min(60, (W - 2 * pad) / divisors.length);
  divisors.forEach((d, i) => {
    const x = (W - dW * divisors.length) / 2 + i * dW + dW / 2;
    svg.append("rect").attr("x", x - dW/2 + 4).attr("y", 130).attr("width", dW - 8).attr("height", 35)
      .attr("fill", "#90caf9").attr("stroke", "#2c5f7c").attr("rx", 4);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", x).attr("y", 153)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "16").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#1f2933")
      .text(d);
  });

  // Show factorization if composite
  if (!isPrime) {
    // smallest non-trivial factor
    let f = 2; while (nVal % f !== 0) f++;
    svg.append("text").attr("x", W/2).attr("y", 195)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "15").attr("fill", "#1f2933").attr("font-family", "monospace")
      .text(`${nVal} = ${f} × ${nVal / f}`);
  }
  return svg.node();
}
```

A **prime** is a number with exactly **two** divisors: 1 and itself. (We
exclude 1, which has only one divisor.) A non-prime ≥ 2 is called
**composite**.

## Wonder

1. Is **1** a prime? Try the widget. What does it say? (1 has only one
   divisor — itself.)
2. Set $n$ to: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13. Notice anything? Now try 4, 6, 8, 9,
   10, 12.
3. Could there be infinitely many primes? Or do they eventually run out?

## Discover

### Problem 1

List all the primes less than 20.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use the widget. Step through 2, 3, 4, ... and write down the ones the
widget marks "PRIME".
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19.** Eight primes below 20.

Note: **2 is the only even prime** — every other even number is divisible
by 2. After 2, primes appear less and less often, and they never settle
into a simple pattern. The "irregularity" of the primes is one of the
deepest mysteries in mathematics.
</details>

### Problem 2

Factor **60** into primes — write 60 as a product of prime numbers.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Start by dividing by the smallest primes. $60 \div 2 = 30$. $30 \div 2
= 15$. $15 \div 3 = 5$. 5 is prime — stop.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$60 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 5$.

Try a different starting point: $60 = 4 \times 15 = (2 \times 2) \times
(3 \times 5) = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5$. **The same primes** —
just in a different order.

> **The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.** Every whole number greater
> than 1 can be written as a product of primes in exactly one way (up to
> the order). Primes are the **building blocks** of all whole numbers.

This is one of mathematics' great theorems — and it is the reason primes
matter at all.
</details>

### Problem 3

Are there infinitely many primes, or do they run out at some big number?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Suppose, for contradiction, that there were only finitely many primes —
say $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k$. What is special about the number
$N = p_1 \times p_2 \times \cdots \times p_k + 1$?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**There are infinitely many primes.** Here is **Euclid's proof** (c. 300
BCE) — one of the most beautiful proofs in mathematics, accessible to a
careful Tier 1 reader.

Suppose for contradiction that there were only finitely many primes,
$p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k$. Consider the number
$$N = p_1 \times p_2 \times \cdots \times p_k + 1.$$

When we divide $N$ by any of the $p_i$, we get **remainder 1** (because
$N$ is exactly $1$ more than a multiple of $p_i$). So *no* $p_i$ divides
$N$ exactly. By the Fundamental Theorem (Problem 2), $N$ must factor
into *some* primes — but those primes can't be in our list. So there
must be primes outside the list, contradicting our assumption.

**Conclusion:** the list of primes can never be complete. There are
infinitely many primes.

This proof, more than 2300 years old, still works. It is a milestone
moment for many learners — the first time they see a *proof of
infinity*. Take a minute. Re-read it.
</details>

### Problem 4

Two friends each pick a prime number. Anu picks **17**. Bina picks
**23**. They tell each other the **product**: $17 \times 23 = 391$.

Now imagine you only know the product (391). Can you recover Anu's and
Bina's numbers?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You'd need to **factor** 391. Try dividing by small primes: $391 \div 2$?
By 3? By 5? ...
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$391 \div 17 = 23$ ✓. So $391 = 17 \times 23$.

For these small primes, factoring takes seconds. **But what if Anu and
Bina had each picked a 300-digit prime?** Their product would be a
600-digit number. *No known algorithm* can factor a random 600-digit
number in any reasonable amount of time — even with all the world's
computers running for the lifetime of the universe.

Multiplying two large primes is **easy**. Factoring the product back is
**hard**. This asymmetry is the mathematical heart of **RSA**, the
encryption used by HTTPS, banking, messaging apps — almost every secure
system on the internet.

When you visit a secure website, your browser and the server are
silently performing this trick at scale. Your security depends on the
mathematical fact you just discovered.
</details>

### Problem 5

Find the **greatest common divisor** (GCD) of **24 and 36** — the
largest number that divides *both*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
List the divisors of each. Find the largest in both lists.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Divisors of 24: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24.
Divisors of 36: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36.
Common divisors: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12. Largest: **12**.

So **GCD(24, 36) = 12**. Equivalently, factor each into primes:
$24 = 2^3 \times 3$, $36 = 2^2 \times 3^2$. Take the *minimum* power of
each shared prime: $2^{\min(3,2)} \times 3^{\min(1,2)} = 2^2 \times 3 = 12$.

GCD is used everywhere: simplifying fractions ($\tfrac{24}{36} =
\tfrac{2}{3}$ via dividing both by 12), scheduling repeating events,
and — in cryptography — the **extended Euclidean algorithm**, which
underpins how RSA generates its keys.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A whole number $a$ is **divisible** by another whole number $b$ if
$a \div b$ has remainder 0 — equivalently, if $a = b \times k$ for some
whole number $k$. We say "$b$ **divides** $a$" and write $b \mid a$.

A **prime** is a whole number $\ge 2$ with exactly **two divisors**: 1
and itself. The first few primes: $2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, \ldots$

A **composite** number is a whole number $\ge 2$ that is *not* prime —
equivalently, has at least three divisors.

The **Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic:** every integer $\ge 2$ has a
unique prime factorisation (up to order).

The **GCD** (greatest common divisor) of two numbers is the largest
number that divides both.

**There are infinitely many primes** (Euclid, c. 300 BCE).

:::

> *Two divisors only — that is a prime;*
> *Every number is a unique product of primes;*
> *Multiplying primes is easy; factoring back is hard;*
> *And on that hardness, the secrets of the modern world rest.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the prime factorisation of **84** and the GCD of **84 and 60**.

A competent solver factors each, then takes the minimum power of each
shared prime:

1. **Factor 84:** $84 \div 2 = 42$. $42 \div 2 = 21$. $21 \div 3 = 7$.
   $7$ is prime. So $84 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 7$.
2. **Factor 60:** $60 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 5$ (from *Discover P2*,
   essentially).
3. **Shared primes:** 2 and 3. Minimum powers: $2^{\min(2,2)} = 2^2$,
   $3^{\min(1,1)} = 3$.
4. **GCD:** $2^2 \times 3 = 12$.

**Answer: $84 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 7$, GCD(84, 60) = 12.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Is each of these prime or composite? **15, 17, 21, 23, 29, 33.**

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Composite: 15 (=3×5), 21 (=3×7), 33 (=3×11). Prime: 17, 23, 29.
</details>

**W2.** Factor into primes: **18, 50, 100.**

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$18 = 2 \times 3^2$. $50 = 2 \times 5^2$. $100 = 2^2 \times 5^2$.
</details>

**W3.** Find GCD: (a) 12 and 18 (b) 20 and 35.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) $12 = 2^2 \times 3$, $18 = 2 \times 3^2$. GCD $= 2 \times 3 = 6$.
(b) $20 = 2^2 \times 5$, $35 = 5 \times 7$. GCD $= 5$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Use the prime factorisation method to *simplify* $\dfrac{42}{56}$
to lowest terms.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Find GCD(42, 56), then divide top and bottom by it.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$42 = 2 \times 3 \times 7$. $56 = 2^3 \times 7$. GCD $= 2 \times 7 = 14$.
$\dfrac{42}{56} = \dfrac{42 \div 14}{56 \div 14} = \dfrac{3}{4}$.

Simplifying fractions (Lesson 7) and finding GCDs are the same task.
</details>

**P2.** A box of **48** chocolates can be evenly distributed in how many
*different* ways (number of friends per box, each getting a whole
number)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each "way" corresponds to a *divisor* of 48.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Divisors of 48: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24, 48 — that's **10
divisors**. (Each divisor is a valid "number of friends".)

Quick way: $48 = 2^4 \times 3$. The number of divisors is
$(4+1)(1+1) = 10$. (The general formula: if $n = p_1^{a_1} \cdots
p_k^{a_k}$, then $n$ has $(a_1+1) \cdots (a_k+1)$ divisors.)
</details>

**P3.** Use the **divisibility shortcuts** from
[Lesson 6 P3](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd) — last digit for 2 and
5; sum of digits for 3 and 9 — to test whether **3,825** is divisible
by 2, 3, 5, 9.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- By **2**: last digit is 5 (odd) → not divisible.
- By **5**: last digit is 5 → divisible. ($3825 \div 5 = 765$.)
- By **3**: $3+8+2+5 = 18$, divisible by 3 → yes. ($3825 \div 3 = 1275$.)
- By **9**: same digit-sum 18, divisible by 9 → yes. ($3825 \div 9 =
  425$.)

These shortcuts are old (the digit-sum rule for 3 is sometimes called
the "rule of nines" and dates to ancient India). They turn many
divisibility questions into one quick calculation.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Bob picks two primes**: $p = 7$, $q = 11$. He computes
$N = p \times q = 77$ and broadcasts $N$ publicly. Alice wants to send
him a private message; she uses $N$ to encrypt it. Anyone listening
sees only the encrypted text and the value 77.

For an attacker to decrypt the message, they need $p$ and $q$
individually. Can you recover them from $N = 77$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$77 = 7 \times 11$. Factoring 77 takes seconds, so the attacker wins —
this is a *toy* example.

In real RSA, $p$ and $q$ are typically **300-digit primes**, so $N$ is
a 600-digit number. No known classical algorithm can factor such an $N$
in less than billions of years. RSA's security is precisely the
**hardness of factoring**.

A note on the future: a sufficiently large **quantum computer** could
factor $N$ quickly using **Shor's algorithm** (1994). This is why
"post-quantum cryptography" — using problems other than factoring —
is an active research area. The lessons in Tier 7 (Encryption track)
will cover this in detail.

The factoring problem you just considered for $N = 77$ is, mathematically,
the same problem the world's most secure systems rest on. Tier 1, the
seed of modern cryptography.
</details>

**S2.** What's the largest prime you can find with no calculator? Try
just *one* number: is **97** prime? (Hint: you only need to check
divisibility by primes $p \le \sqrt{97} \approx 9.8$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You only need to test primes ≤ $\sqrt{97}$ — that's just 2, 3, 5, 7.
(Why? If $97 = a \times b$ with $a, b > 1$, the smaller of $a, b$ is at
most $\sqrt{97}$.)
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Check by 2: no (97 is odd). By 3: digit sum $9 + 7 = 16$, not divisible
by 3. By 5: doesn't end in 0 or 5. By 7: $7 \times 13 = 91$, $7 \times
14 = 98$ — no. So **97 is prime.**

The "$p \le \sqrt{n}$" trick is a *huge* speedup. Without it, you'd test
all the way up to 97. With it, you only test up to 9. This idea —
**bounded search using square roots** — is everywhere in computer
algorithms.

The largest *known* prime as of writing has more than 24 million digits
(it is a *Mersenne prime*, $2^{82{,}589{,}933} - 1$, found in 2018).
The hunt for primes is a major collaborative computing project (GIMPS).
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"1 is a prime."** No — by convention, primes are *at least 2* and
   have *exactly two* divisors. 1 has only one. This convention is
   chosen so the Fundamental Theorem (unique factorisation) works
   cleanly.
2. **"Prime numbers are rare."** They become *less dense* as numbers
   grow, but there are *infinitely* many (Euclid, *Discover P3*). About
   $\tfrac{n}{\ln n}$ primes lie below $n$ — a beautiful estimate from
   the Prime Number Theorem (Tier 5+).
3. **"You can find prime factors quickly."** For small numbers, yes.
   For 600-digit numbers, no — and the security of the internet depends
   on this fact.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state Euclid's proof that there are infinitely many primes,
   in your own words?
2. Without scrolling back, can you factor 84 into primes?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $84 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 7$.)

- **3 yes:** **You have completed Tier 1 — Foundations!** The next step
  is the [Tier 1 index](../index.qmd) for a recap, or pick a goal from
  one of the [applied bridges](../../../applied-bridges/encryption.qmd).
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** — the encryption-asymmetry idea is *the* prize
  of this lesson. Then proceed.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P3*. The Fundamental Theorem
  and Euclid's proof are the heart of the lesson.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](../06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)** —
  divisibility *is* the question of zero remainder.

### What this unlocks

This is the **last lesson of Tier 1**. Going forward:

- **Tier 5 — Number Theory** — modular arithmetic, Fermat's little
  theorem, the Euclidean algorithm in full generality, the Prime
  Number Theorem.
- **Tier 7 — Encryption track** — RSA, Diffie–Hellman, elliptic curves,
  lattice-based post-quantum cryptography. All built on the foundation
  laid here.
- **Tier 7 — Research / number theory** — analytic number theory, the
  Riemann hypothesis (the most famous unsolved problem in mathematics
  is about the distribution of primes).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** Every secure connection on the internet (HTTPS, SSH,
TLS, signed software, blockchain transactions, encrypted messaging) is
built on the hardness of factoring large products of primes (RSA), or
on closely related problems (discrete log, elliptic curves). The
problem you just considered for $N = 77$ is the *same* problem the
world's secrets rest on.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Number theory is one of the oldest and
deepest branches of mathematics. The distribution of primes touches
analysis (Riemann zeta function), algebra (algebraic number theory),
and even physics (random matrix theory). The most famous unsolved
problem in mathematics — the *Riemann hypothesis*, with a $1 million
prize — is about primes.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Computer science.** Hash functions, error-correcting codes, random
number generators, distributed systems consensus protocols (e.g.
Schoenmakers's threshold signatures), and many more all use number
theory. Knuth devotes most of *The Art of Computer Programming* Volume
2 to this.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** ISBN check digits, credit card validation (Luhn
algorithm), CRC error detection in network protocols, even some
musical scales — divisibility ideas appear in surprisingly many
practical engineering systems.
:::

---

## You have finished Tier 1

This lesson closes Tier 1 — the **Foundations** of the curriculum.
Across 15 lessons you have re-met:

1. What a number is.
2. Where numbers live (the line).
3. How we name them (place value).
4. The four operations, re-grounded as walks and rectangles.
5. Fractions, decimals, ratios, and negative numbers — the
   *extensions* that make arithmetic complete.
6. Variables and equations — the *language* of mathematics.
7. Geometry — shapes, angles, and measurements.
8. Statistics — averages, the language of data.
9. Number theory — the seed of modern encryption.

You are now ready for **Tier 2 — Pre-Algebra & Algebra I (Grade 8–9)**,
or for any of the applied tracks. The dependency graph is in
[`_curriculum.yml`](/_curriculum.yml); the visual map is on
the [home page](/index.qmd).

Congratulations.

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the divisor-display widget
  makes prime/composite visible at a glance), Boole (the *what makes
  primes special?* question precedes the definition), Russian school
  (Euclid's proof in *Discover P3* is presented as a *real* proof, not
  hand-waved), Bhāskara (single-box Name + verse — and India is the
  origin of much of early number theory, from Brahmagupta to Aryabhata
  to the Kerala school), Better Explained (the "Aha!" framing is
  *multiply-easy / factor-hard, and that's why the internet is
  secure*).
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
